title: Triple-tier macramé plant hanger
subtitle: Three stacked pot cradles in a single hanging piece
excerpt: Three pot cradles stacked vertically on one set of hanging cords, sized to hold pots of graduating diameter. Built from alternating square knots with gathering knots to form each pocket. Spacing between cradles is adjustable by measuring the knot-free cord sections. A good next project for makers who have completed a single-pot hanger.
sourceNotes:
- General macramé practice: tiered plant hanger construction, alternating square knot cradle pattern
- Pot sizing guidance: standard terracotta pot diameter ranges for 3-tier proportions

glossaryTerms.definition: A symmetrical flat knot made over two central filler cords using two working cords; forms the basic unit of most macramé fabric.
A pattern in which adjacent square knots share filler cords from neighbouring knots, creating an offset grid that produces an open, net-like cradle structure.
A wrapping knot that binds a group of cords together tightly; used at the base of each cradle to secure the pot and between cradle sections to create spacing.
A simple mounting knot used to attach cords to a ring at their midpoint.
The passive central cord(s) over which working cords are knotted; holds shape and tension.

content.text: A triple-tier plant hanger uses one set of hanging cords running from top to bottom, with three cradle sections built at measured intervals along the cords. The top cradle is the smallest (holding the smallest pot) and the bottom cradle is the largest. Each cradle uses
alternating square knots
to form a net pocket, closed beneath the pot with a
gathering knot
. Between cradles, the cords hang free and can be spaced to any length you prefer. The piece uses 5 mm single-strand cotton cord; approximately 60 m total.
Setup
Cut 8 cords each 7.5 m long. Fold each cord in half and mount all 8 onto a 15 cm metal ring using
lark's head knots

. You now have 16 working ends hanging from the ring. Hang the ring from an S-hook at a comfortable working height. Divide the 16 cords into 4 groups of 4 cords; within each group, the 2 outer cords are
working cords
and the 2 inner are
filler cords
Method
Step 1: Work a top knot column. On each of the 4 groups, tie 4-5
square knots
close together, starting approximately 8 cm below the ring.

Step 2: Build cradle 1 (top, smallest pot). Leave 4-5 cm of bare cord below the square-knot column on each group (this forms the sides of the net). Then work one round of alternating square knots: take 2 cords from the right of one group and 2 cords from the left of the adjacent group and tie a square knot. Repeat around all 4 pairs. This opens the cradle structure into a net. Work 2-3 rounds of alternating square knots for the cradle depth.
Step 3: Close cradle 1. Bring all 16 cords together and bind tightly with a gathering knot, wrapping 8-10 times. This forms the base that the pot sits on.

Step 4: Measure the spacing section. Below the first gathering knot, leave a measured length of bare cord for the hanging section between tier 1 and tier 2. A typical spacing is 20-25 cm for a medium hanger; adjust to suit your ceiling height and pot sizes.
Step 5: Build cradle 2 (middle, medium pot). Repeat Steps 1-3, but make the cradle 1-2 rows deeper and leave slightly more bare cord in the sides to accommodate the larger pot diameter.
Step 6: Repeat the spacing section (Step 4), then build cradle 3 (bottom, largest pot) following the same pattern with a deeper cradle to match the largest pot.

Step 7: Final gathering knot and fringe. Below cradle 3, bring all 16 cords together and bind with a final gathering knot. Leave 20-25 cm of fringe below; trim the ends straight or cut them into a tapered point.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Pot slips through the cradle base

content.text: The gathering knot is too loose or the alternating square knot rows are too open. Retie the gathering knot more tightly. For a deeper cradle next time, add another round of alternating square knots before closing.

attrs.problem: Cradles are not evenly spaced along the hanger

content.text: Measure the bare cord section from the gathering knot before tying the next square knot column; do not estimate by eye. Mark the planned position with a T-pin before starting the next square knot section.

attrs.problem: Cords run out before all three cradles are complete

content.text: The cords were cut too short. As a rule, each cord should be at least 4 times the planned finished length of the hanger, plus extra for each cradle. For a hanger with 3 cradles totalling 80 cm in length, start with cords of at least 7 m.
